channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - wccftech.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for wccftech.com

item.title: The Gate Must Stand Review – Break Down the Walls
item.content:encoded:
Game Info
The Gate Must Stand

18th June, 2026

Publisher

Gamersky Games, Yogscast Games

Normally, when writing a review - or almost anything - I would start with a little blurb or story. Maybe it's about the genre, maybe the developer, maybe it's just some random anecdote that the game has made me think of. With The Gate Must Stand, I've got nothing. Possibly something about the Matt Damon film The Great Wall. Why? Because there's a wall in it. That's about it. Anyway, after that random detour to a film I now want to watch again, let's talk about the game.

Let's start by saying The Gate Must Stand is the fifty-second game published by Gamersky on Steam in 2026. Before this, I had no clue what Gamersky was. Having read up about it, being one of the biggest sites and gaming communities in China, the trend I can see makes sense. Find a Chinese game, do a quick translation of said game, and dump it on Steam. Your quality may vary.

In terms of quality, The Gate Must Stand isn't bad by any sense of the word. It's polished and, for the most part, runs well. The translations aren't terrible, even if things are a little wordy at times, and I'm at the point where I have absolutely no clue what some of the boosts do, are or even if they are real and not some mushroom-induced fever dream.

There are times when the game starts to chug if there are too many enemies on screen, and your defenders are firing off more effects than bonfire night. I won't hold it against the game too much, as it was few and far between. There are other things I have niggles about, though. Most of all, the game doesn't make it easy to remotely understand what in the blue hell is happening when it comes to levelling up one of your followers, or even your character.

I should explain first, this is a tower defence game, but the towers are characters like an ice mage, fire mage, guardian - essentially some specialist class from an RPG. You can level these up by buying more and stacking them, up to a maximum level of ten. This also isn't like general tower defence titles, where there are specific paths. Here, there's one wider path, and you can filter the monsters by putting down barricades.

Back to levelling. Every time you level one of your towers, or you level yourself by collecting experience dropped by dead enemies, you can pick between one of three random upgrades. What I'm finding irritating is that while an upgrade results in a large number of sub-upgrades, some of them can be levelled up, and some seem useless to me. Maybe they're not, and just didn't suit my focus, or maybe I got bored of trying to figure out what some of them do with their absurdly long descriptions.

The same issue also applies to relics, which can be obtained through quests that appear throughout a run and dropped from bosses that appear every five levels. I suppose what I'm really saying is that it feels like there's too much, and a fair amount feels superfluous and either added because this is a random roguelite, meaning not every upgrade can be useful, or the devs felt 'more is better' in The Gate Must Stand, when that isn't always the case.

What I will say is that the loop itself is satisfying. You start a run with three stages, and your aim is to reach the end and win the last stage. You do this by placing your towers, running around to attack monsters with your character, and using up to two activatable skills. You pick up the experience they drop and spend the money you get, which I haven't figured out if it is through killing or just dropped periodically, on the towers and upgrades from a merchant that periodically appears. So far, so active-tower-defence.

If you do manage to get to the end, a run will take you sixty minutes, though speeding the game up and just pausing to plop down or level up your tower is the best option to make it that bit quicker. Win or lose, you'll get some magical coins that can then be spent on permanent upgrades to make your later runs that bit easier. Once you win on a difficulty, you can move up to the next, where the enemies will be that bit harder to beat.

All in all, I don't mind The Gate Must Stand. The gameplay loop and the replay value from increased difficulties, unlocked new followers, finding other relics, and fighting different bosses will give fans of this type of game enough to keep going. The replay value is there, with these new ways of defending yourself and different methods you can use to set up the routes.

However, there is something about this that doesn't let me outright say "this is a good game". I can't point to one thing and say "it's this", but it's just an overall feeling. The gameplay is fine, though the presentation is lacking. The UI could have been better, with text-dumps hurting it further. The enemies can get stuck on the map, your towers, or barricades. The less-than-bare-bones narrative hurts it, too, and the pacing is on the slow side, which is why I recommend setting it to 2x speed.

All in all, The Gate Must Stand is competent, but it's not special. It's not even as good as Senmu Studio's first title. Sometimes, competent is fine. In this case, with the game costing only £7.99 at full price, with a 30% discount at launch, that'll do. I've spent more on a sandwich meal deal and had a worse experience. It's fine, and I may play it a bit more. Take that as you will.

Copy provided by the publisher.

You can find additional information about our standard review process and ethics policy here.

5

WCCFTECH RATING

The Gate Must Stand

The Gate Must Stand is a passable tower defence game with reasonable gameplay and decent replay value, though it can be undermined by poor pacing, UI and item descriptions that need improvement. While not a bad game, it's also not great. Reasonably cheap on a launch offer, it will sufficiently pass time and be fine doing it.

    Pros
  • Replay value due to roguelite elements like progression
  • Reasonable tactical options within the combat of the game...
    Cons
  • ... albeit undermined by poor pacing, and what feel like a variety of superfluous upgrades/elements
  • No real narrative hook, or setting, to draw you in
  • UI could be better, with text dumps making that worse
Buy for from Amazon The links above are affiliate links. As an Amazon Associate, Wccftech.com may earn from qualifying purchases.

About the author: Chris Wray has been writing at Wccftech gaming section since 2015 and is an opinionated bloke from the north of the UK (think Ned Stark). He enjoys video games, films, books, beer, whisky and other alcohol. He also supports Manchester Utd and for some reason he writes profile pages in the third person. His expertise is in gaming and the games industry, primarily on the PC. In addition to this, he works with and contributes to the finance and tech sections.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/review/the-gate-must-stand-review-against-all-odds/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Where Winds Meet Developer Q&A: Hidden Mountain’s Vertical Exploration, Future Roadmap, and Ongoing Netcode and Performance Optimization
item.content:encoded:
The popular free-to-play open world Wuxia action RPG Where Winds Meet, already available on PC, PlayStation 5, Android and iOS, launched on Xbox Series S and X earlier this month, featuring exclusive benefits for Game Pass subscribers. The announcement also included a tease for the game's third major expansion, titled Hidden Mountain and slated for a July launch.

Following the news, we had the opportunity to send a list of questions to developer Everstone Studio via publisher NetEase Games to learn more about the development of the Xbox version and the upcoming Hidden Mountain expansion, and to address other pain points of the live service game that have been highlighted by the community so far. You can find the answers from the Head of Publishing, Eric Zheng, below.

By the way, the development team has just announced that another content update, version 1.8, will launch on June 25, introducing a Homestead System and interactive pets, such as cats and geese, which will be featured in a dedicated pet battle system at a later time.

How long have you been working on the Xbox version? Was it challenging to optimize Where Winds Meet for the less powerful Xbox Series S?

Eric Zheng: Development of the XBOX version began alongside our broader console platform planning. As Where Winds Meet features a vast open world, multiplayer interactions, and extensive real-time systems, our goal has always been to deliver the same high-quality experience to XBOX players as on other platforms. As a result, platform adaptation and optimization have been integrated throughout the development process.

Beyond the core porting work, the team invested significant effort in performance tuning, control adaptation, and platform-specific features to ensure the game fully takes advantage of the capabilities of XBOX Series S|X hardware.

Optimizing for XBOX Series S presented its own challenges. Where Winds Meet features complex environments, a rich ecosystem, and multiplayer gameplay, requiring us to carefully balance visual quality, memory usage, and overall stability.

That said, Series S benefits from a modern console architecture. Our tech team has implemented targeted optimizations across asset streaming, scene rendering, memory management, and overall performance. Through extensive refinement, we have been able to deliver the core open-world wuxia experience of Where Winds Meet on XBOX Series S while maintaining a stable and enjoyable gameplay experience.

Will Xbox players have any way to access previously limited-time or gacha items?

Eric Zheng: We are continuously listening to and evaluating the needs and preferences of different player groups. For information regarding future event plans, rerun schedules, and acquisition methods, please keep an eye on our official announcements. Thank you for your patience and support!

What can you share about the upcoming Hidden Mountain expansion and its new mechanics and gameplay loops?

Eric Zheng: The Hidden Mountain expansion will launch in July, and we unveiled its first trailer during our Xbox release on June 8. Unlike previous regions, this is our first attempt to explore and showcase the beauty of cliffs and summits. To bring this vision to life, we've significantly enhanced the region's atmosphere and environmental detail through three core elements: wind, light, and stone. Dappled sunlight dances across rugged mountainsides, the wind echoes through the forests, and ancient stone paths bear the marks of time. Together, these elements help create a world that feels tangible, immersive, and alive.

From a gameplay perspective, we're aiming to provide players with genuinely new experiences. The unique terrain of The Hidden Mountain region means we aren't confined to a flat plane; instead, we can expand exploration vertically, crafting a multi-layered experience.

If Hexi was a tribute to timeless poetry and painting, then with Bujian Mountain, we want to honor the ancient sages' pursuit of reason and ultimate 'truth.' This expansion explores a deeper theme: humanity's pursuit of wisdom and knowledge. Throughout history, countless people have devoted their lives to seeking answers. Whether their conclusions proved right or wrong, their pursuit of knowledge has continued to push humanity forward, inspiring generations across time and expanding the boundaries of what we believe is possible.

We'll be sharing more details about the new Mohist Sect, upcoming new campaign and gaming mechanisms, as well as powerful new enemies in the near future.

Following this expansion, what are your general goals for future Where Winds Meet content?

Eric Zheng: Our goal is to establish Where Winds Meet as an open-world wuxia experience with long-term appeal. We will continue expanding the main storyline and the stories of major factions, while leading players into unexplored regions filled with new adventures, mysteries, and unexpected Jianghu encounters.

At the same time, we plan to further enrich exploration, social features, and multiplayer experiences across the open world. We want this Jianghu to offer not only compelling stories but also a constant sense of discovery and meaningful reasons for players to keep returning to their journey.

Will there be plans to make everything dyeable, including accessories and F2P items?

Eric Zheng: When deciding which items are suitable for the dye system, we need to carefully consider factors such as visual presentation, design intent, and the overall player experience.

That said, customization and self-expression remain important priorities for us. We will continue listening to player feedback and exploring new ways to expand appearance customization and give players more freedom to express their personal style.

Are there any plans to reduce the storage size of Where Winds Meet in the future? How much larger is the game expected to get on mobile, especially for players on older devices like the iPhone 12 Pro Max?

Eric Zheng: As Where Winds Meet continues to grow, we'll be introducing new regions, story content, and gameplay features, which means the overall installation size may increase over time.

At the same time, we're aware of players' concerns about storage space. We will continue optimizing resource management and download solutions to help minimize storage requirements while still expanding the game's scope and content.

As for future file size estimates, we don't have any finalized figures to share at this time. We'll provide more details once development progresses further and those plans become more concrete.

Can you clarify the Black Market situation for the global version, including whether it will be added, replaced, or omitted entirely, and what that means for past banner outfits and unopened cosmetic items?

Eric Zheng: We've seen many requests from players who would like to see the Black Market system become available in the global version.

However, due to differences in legal and compliance requirements across various regions worldwide, we are currently evaluating alternative systems that would be better suited for the global player experience. We'll share more information as development progresses.

What is Everstone Studio doing to improve netcode and server performance, especially for PvP and co-op? Are there plans to reduce the dependency on ping and improve regional server support?

Eric Zheng: To support our growing global community, we continue to invest in server infrastructure, network stability, and multiplayer improvements.

We have already launched dedicated servers in Oceania, the Middle East, and South America, helping deliver lower latency and more stable connections for players in those regions. Looking ahead, we'll continue evaluating future regional expansion opportunities based on player distribution, community feedback, and operational considerations.

For PvP and co-op content, we're also continuously improving matchmaking and network performance. For example, we recently introduced cross-server matchmaking for the Arena. Players can choose whether to enable this feature. When queue times become too long on a player's home server, the system can expand matchmaking across additional servers, helping reduce wait times while maintaining match quality.

Minimizing the impact of network conditions on gameplay remains a long-term focus for us. We'll continue refining our networking technology, server stability, and multiplayer systems to deliver a smoother and fairer experience for players around the world.

Is there any chance of a Mac launch? What about Nintendo Switch 2?

Eric Zheng: We are actively evaluating opportunities to bring Where Winds Meet to additional platforms. We'll share more information at an appropriate time.

Will console performance, including PS5 Pro, be further optimized?

Eric Zheng: Absolutely. Performance optimization is an ongoing effort for us. On PS5 Pro, we plan to continue improving overall performance through enhancements such as Direct I/O-based resource loading, reduced memory usage peaks, improved stability, and shorter loading times.

On XBOX, our current focus includes shader optimizations to reduce memory consumption and improve stability, presentation pipeline improvements to lower input and display latency while delivering more consistent frame rates, and DirectStorage enhancements to further optimize asset streaming, reduce memory overhead, and accelerate loading speeds.

As Where Winds Meet continues to grow with new content and features, we will keep monitoring performance across all platforms and make further improvements based on player feedback and real-world performance data.

Thank you for your time.

About the author: With over two decades of experience in gaming journalism, Alessio Palumbo has led the gaming vertical at Wccftech since August 2015. He started working at a young age for Italian websites like Everyeye.it, Gamestar.it, Nextgame.it, and Multiplayer.it before kickstarting the indie English-language publication Worlds Factory as its founder and Editor in Chief. In the last decade, he has coordinated the overall output of Wccftech's gaming section, managed PR relations, assigned reviews, produced daily news coverage, edited gaming content as needed, and delivered game reviews. Arguably, his trademark content is the long series of exclusive developer interviews that have been cited by Wikipedia and by the biggest news media and gaming publications. His passion for technology also makes him knowledgeable when it comes to gaming hardware and tech. His favorite genres include RPGs, MMORPGs, and action/adventure games.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/where-winds-meet-interview-hidden-mountain/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
